# densityratio

## Overview

This package provides functionality to directly estimate a density ratio
``` math
r(x) = \frac{p_\text{nu}(x)}{p_{\text{de}}(x)},
```
without estimating the numerator and denominator density separately.
Density ratio estimation serves many purposes, for example, prediction,
outlier detection, change-point detection in time-series, importance
weighting under domain adaptation (i.e., sample selection bias) and
evaluation of synthetic data utility. The key idea is that differences
between data distributions can be captured in their density ratio, which
is estimated over the entire multivariate space of the data.
Subsequently, the density ratio values can be used to summarize the
dissimilarity between the two distributions in a discrepancy measure.

![](reference/figures/README-densities-1.png)

### Features

- **Fast**: Computationally intensive code is executed in `C++` using
  `Rcpp` and `RcppArmadillo`.
- **Automatic**: Good default hyperparameters that can be optimized in
  cross-validation (we do recommend understanding those parameters
  before using `densityratio` in practice).
- **Complete**: Several density ratio estimation methods, such as
  unconstrained least-squares importance fitting
  ([`ulsif()`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/ulsif.md)),
  Kullback-Leibler importance estimation procedure
  ([`kliep()`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/kliep.md)),
  kernel mean matching
  ([`kmm()`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/kmm.md)),
  ratio of estimated densities
  ([`naive()`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/naive.md)),
  spectral density ratio estimation
  ([`spectral()`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/spectral.md)
  and least-squares heterodistributional subspace search
  ([`lhss()`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/lhss.md)).
- **User-friendly**: Simple user interface, default
  [`predict()`](https://rdrr.io/r/stats/predict.html),
  [`print()`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/print.html) and
  [`summary()`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/summary.html) functions for all
  density ratio estimation methods; built-in data sets for quick
  testing.

## Installation

You can install the stable release of `densityratio` from
[CRAN](https://CRAN.R-project.org/package=densityratio) with:

``` r

install.packages('densityratio')
```

For the development version, use:

``` r

remotes::install_github("thomvolker/densityratio")
```

## Usage

The package contains several functions to estimate the density ratio
between the numerator data and the denominator data. To illustrate the
functionality, we make use of the in-built simulated data sets
`numerator_data` and `denominator_data`, that both consist of the same
five variables.

### Minimal example

``` r

library(densityratio)

set.seed(11)

head(numerator_data)
#> # A tibble: 6 × 5
#>   x1    x2         x3     x4     x5
#>   <fct> <fct>   <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>
#> 1 A     G1    -0.0299  0.967 -1.26 
#> 2 C     G1     2.29   -0.475  2.40 
#> 3 A     G1     1.37    0.577 -0.172
#> 4 B     G2     1.44   -0.193 -0.708
#> 5 A     G1     1.01    2.23   2.01 
#> 6 C     G2     1.83    0.762  3.71

fit <- ulsif(
  df_numerator = numerator_data$x5,
  df_denominator = denominator_data$x5,
  nsigma = 5,
  nlambda = 5
)

class(fit)
#> [1] "ulsif"
```

We can ask for the [`summary()`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/summary.html) of
the estimated density ratio object, that contains the optimal kernel
weights (optimized using cross-validation) and a measure of discrepancy
between the numerator and denominator densities.

``` r

summary(fit)
#> 
#> Call:
#> ulsif(df_numerator = numerator_data$x5, df_denominator = denominator_data$x5,     nsigma = 5, nlambda = 5)
#> 
#> Kernel Information:
#>   Kernel type: Gaussian with L2 norm distances
#>   Number of kernels: 200
#> 
#> Optimal sigma: 0.7157604
#> Optimal lambda: 0.03162278
#> Optimal kernel weights: num [1:200] -0.02582 0.00116 -0.01221 0.01429 -0.03272 ...
#>  
#> Pearson divergence between P(nu) and P(de): 0.2905
#> For a two-sample homogeneity test, use 'summary(x, test = TRUE)'.
```

To formally evaluate whether the numerator and denominator densities
differ significantly, you can perform a two-sample homogeneity test as
follows.

``` r

summary(fit, test = TRUE)
#> 
#> Call:
#> ulsif(df_numerator = numerator_data$x5, df_denominator = denominator_data$x5,     nsigma = 5, nlambda = 5)
#> 
#> Kernel Information:
#>   Kernel type: Gaussian with L2 norm distances
#>   Number of kernels: 200
#> 
#> Optimal sigma: 0.7157604
#> Optimal lambda: 0.03162278
#> Optimal kernel weights: num [1:200] -0.02582 0.00116 -0.01221 0.01429 -0.03272 ...
#>  
#> Pearson divergence between P(nu) and P(de): 0.2905
#> Pr(P(nu)=P(de)) < .001
#> Bonferroni-corrected for testing with r(x) = P(nu)/P(de) AND r*(x) = P(de)/P(nu).
```

The probability that numerator and denominator samples share a common
data generating mechanism is very small.

The `ulsif`-object also contains the (hyper-)parameters used in
estimating the density ratio, such as the centers used in constructing
the Gaussian kernels (`fit$centers`), the different bandwidth parameters
(`fit$sigma`) and the regularization parameters (`fit$lambda`). Using
these variables, we can obtain the estimated density ratio using
[`predict()`](https://rdrr.io/r/stats/predict.html).

``` r

# obtain predictions for the numerator samples

newx5 <- seq(from = -3, to = 6, by = 0.05)
pred <- predict(fit, newdata = newx5)

ggplot() +
  geom_point(aes(x = newx5, y = pred, col = "ulsif estimates")) +
  stat_function(
    mapping = aes(col = "True density ratio"),
    fun = dratio,
    args = list(p = 0.4, dif = 3, mu = 3, sd = 2),
    linewidth = 1
  ) +
  theme_classic() +
  scale_color_manual(name = NULL, values = c("#de0277", "purple")) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "inside",
    legend.position.inside = c(0.8, 0.9),
    text = element_text(size = 50),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 50),
  )
```

![](reference/figures/README-plot-univ-1.png)

### Scaling

By default, all functions in the `densityratio` package standardize the
data to the numerator means and standard deviations. This is done to
ensure that the importance of each variable in the kernel estimates is
not dependent on the scale of the data. By setting
`scale = "denominator"` one can scale the data to the means and standard
deviations of the denominator data, and by setting `scale = FALSE` the
data remains on the original scale.

### Categorical data

All of the functions in the `densityratio` package accept categorical
variables types. However, note that internally, these variables are
one-hot encoded, which can lead to a high-dimensional feature-space.

``` r

summary(numerator_data$x1)
#>   A   B   C 
#> 351 339 310
summary(denominator_data$x1)
#>   A   B   C 
#> 252 232 516

fit_cat <- ulsif(
  df_numerator = numerator_data$x1,
  df_denominator = denominator_data$x1
)
#> Warning in check.sigma(nsigma, sigma_quantile, sigma, dist_nu): There are duplicate values in 'sigma', only the unique values are used.

aggregate(
  predict(fit_cat) ~ numerator_data$x1,
  FUN = unique
)
#>   numerator_data$x1 predict(fit_cat)
#> 1                 A        1.3679015
#> 2                 B        1.4331335
#> 3                 C        0.6111301

table(numerator_data$x1) / table(denominator_data$x1)
#> 
#>         A         B         C 
#> 1.3928571 1.4612069 0.6007752
```

This transformation can give a reasonable estimate of the ratio of
proportions in the different data sets (although there is some
regularization applied such that the estimated odds are closer to one
than seen in the real data).

### Full data example

After transforming all variables to numeric variables, it is possible to
calculate the density ratio over the entire multivariate space of the
data.

``` r

fit_all <- ulsif(
  df_numerator = numerator_data,
  df_denominator = denominator_data
)

summary(fit_all, test = TRUE, parallel = TRUE)
#> 
#> Call:
#> ulsif(df_numerator = numerator_data, df_denominator = denominator_data)
#> 
#> Kernel Information:
#>   Kernel type: Gaussian with L2 norm distances
#>   Number of kernels: 200
#> 
#> Optimal sigma: 2.206548
#> Optimal lambda: 0.001
#> Optimal kernel weights: num [1:200] 0.976 -1.336 -0.232 0.348 -0.528 ...
#>  
#> Pearson divergence between P(nu) and P(de): 1.025
#> Pr(P(nu)=P(de)) < .001
#> Bonferroni-corrected for testing with r(x) = P(nu)/P(de) AND r*(x) = P(de)/P(nu).
```

### Other density ratio estimation functions

Besides
[`ulsif()`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/ulsif.md),
the package contains several other functions to estimate a density
ratio.

- [`naive()`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/naive.md)
  estimates the numerator and denominator densities separately, and
  subsequently takes there ratio.
- [`kliep()`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/kliep.md)
  estimates the density ratio directly through the Kullback-Leibler
  importance estimation procedure.
- [`kmm()`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/kmm.md)
  estimates the density ratio through kernel mean matching.
- [`lhss()`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/lhss.md)
  estimates the density ratio in a subspace where the two distributions
  are most different using least-squares heterodistributional subspace
  search.
- [`spectral()`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/spectral.md)
  estimates the density ratio using a spectral series approach.

We display
[`kliep()`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/kliep.md)
and
[`naive()`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/naive.md)
as examples here. The other functions are discussed in the [Get Started
vignette](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/articles/densityratio.html).

``` r

fit_naive <- naive(
  df_numerator = numerator_data$x5,
  df_denominator = denominator_data$x5
)

fit_kliep <- kliep(
  df_numerator = numerator_data$x5,
  df_denominator = denominator_data$x5
)


pred_naive <- predict(fit_naive, newdata = newx5)
pred_kliep <- predict(fit_kliep, newdata = newx5)


ggplot(data = NULL, aes(x = newx5)) +
  geom_point(aes(y = pred, col = "ulsif estimates")) +
  geom_point(aes(y = pred_naive, col = "naive estimates")) +
  geom_point(aes(y = pred_kliep, col = "kliep estimates")) +
  stat_function(aes(x = NULL, col = "True density ratio"),
    fun = dratio, args = list(p = 0.4, dif = 3, mu = 3, sd = 2),
    linewidth = 1
  ) +
  theme_classic() +
  scale_color_manual(name = NULL, values = c("pink", "#512970", "#de0277", "purple")) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "inside",
    legend.position.inside = c(0.8, 0.9),
    text = element_text(size = 50),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 50)
  )
```

![](reference/figures/README-plot-methods-1.png)

The figure directly shows that
[`ulsif()`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/ulsif.md)
and
[`kliep()`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/kliep.md)
are quite stable and come rather close to the true density ratio
function in this example. The
[`naive()`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/naive.md)
solution is slightly more wiggly and less accurate than the other
methods.

## Contributions

This package is still in development, and I’ll be happy to take feedback
and suggestions. Please submit these through [GitHub
Issues](https://github.com/thomvolker/densityratio/issues).

## Resources

**Books**

- General information about the density ratio estimation framework:
  Sugiyama, Suzuki and Kanamori (2012). [Density Ratio Estimation in
  Machine
  Learning](https://www.cambridge.org/core/books/density-ratio-estimation-in-machine-learning/BCBEA6AEAADD66569B1E85DDDEAA7648)

**Papers**

- Density ratio estimation for the evaluation of the utility of
  synthetic data: Volker, De Wolf and Van Kesteren (2024). [A density
  ratio framework for evaluating the utility of synthetic
  data](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2408.13167)

- Density ratio estimation for covariate shift: Huang, Smola, Gretton,
  Borgwardt and Schölkopf (2007). [Correcting sample selection bias by
  unlabeled
  data](https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper/2006/file/a2186aa7c086b46ad4e8bf81e2a3a19b-Paper.pdf)

- High-dimensional density ratio estimation through a spectral series
  approach: Izbicki, Lee and Schafer (2014). [High-dimensional density
  ratio estimation with extensions to approximate likelihood
  computation](https://proceedings.mlr.press/v33/izbicki14.pdf)

- Least-squares density ratio estimation: Sugiyama, Hido and Kanamori
  (2009). [A least-squares approach to direct importance
  estimation](https://www.jmlr.org/papers/volume10/kanamori09a/kanamori09a.pdf)

## How to cite

Volker, T.B., Poses, C. & Van Kesteren, E.J. (2023). densityratio:
Distribution comparison through density ratio estimation.
<https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.8307818>

# Package index

## All functions

- [`colon`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/colon.md)
  : colon

- [`create_bivariate_plot()`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/create_bivariate_plot.md)
  : Bivariate plot

- [`create_univariate_plot()`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/create_univariate_plot.md)
  : Univariate plot

- [`denominator_data`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/denominator_data.md)
  : denominator_data

- [`denominator_small`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/denominator_small.md)
  : denominator_small

- [`dr.histogram()`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/dr.histogram.md)
  [`plot(`*`<ulsif>`*`)`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/dr.histogram.md)
  [`plot(`*`<kliep>`*`)`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/dr.histogram.md)
  [`plot(`*`<kmm>`*`)`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/dr.histogram.md)
  [`plot(`*`<spectral>`*`)`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/dr.histogram.md)
  [`plot(`*`<lhss>`*`)`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/dr.histogram.md)
  [`plot(`*`<naivedensityratio>`*`)`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/dr.histogram.md)
  : A histogram of density ratio estimates

- [`insurance`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/insurance.md)
  : insurance

- [`kidiq`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/kidiq.md)
  : kidiq

- [`kliep()`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/kliep.md)
  : Kullback-Leibler importance estimation procedure

- [`kmm()`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/kmm.md)
  : Kernel mean matching approach to density ratio estimation

- [`lhss()`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/lhss.md)
  : Least-squares heterodistributional subspace search

- [`naive()`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/naive.md)
  : Naive density ratio estimation

- [`numerator_data`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/numerator_data.md)
  : numerator_data

- [`numerator_small`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/numerator_small.md)
  : numerator_small

- [`permute()`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/permute.md)
  : Single permutation

- [`plot_bivariate()`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/plot_bivariate.md)
  : Densityratio in two-dimensional plot

- [`plot_univariate()`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/plot_univariate.md)
  : Scatter plot of density ratios and individual variables

- [`predict(`*`<kliep>`*`)`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/predict.kliep.md)
  :

  Obtain predicted density ratio values from a `kliep` object

- [`predict(`*`<kmm>`*`)`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/predict.kmm.md)
  :

  Obtain predicted density ratio values from a `kmm` object

- [`predict(`*`<lhss>`*`)`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/predict.lhss.md)
  :

  Obtain predicted density ratio values from a `lhss` object

- [`predict(`*`<naivedensityratio>`*`)`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/predict.naivedensityratio.md)
  :

  Obtain predicted density ratio values from a `naivedensityratio`
  object

- [`predict(`*`<spectral>`*`)`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/predict.spectral.md)
  :

  Obtain predicted density ratio values from a `spectral` object

- [`predict(`*`<ulsif>`*`)`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/predict.ulsif.md)
  :

  Obtain predicted density ratio values from a `ulsif` object

- [`print(`*`<kliep>`*`)`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/print.kliep.md)
  :

  Print a `kliep` object

- [`print(`*`<kmm>`*`)`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/print.kmm.md)
  :

  Print a `kmm` object

- [`print(`*`<lhss>`*`)`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/print.lhss.md)
  :

  Print a `lhss` object

- [`print(`*`<naivedensityratio>`*`)`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/print.naivedensityratio.md)
  :

  Print a `naivedensityratio` object

- [`print(`*`<spectral>`*`)`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/print.spectral.md)
  :

  Print a `spectral` object

- [`print(`*`<summary.kliep>`*`)`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/print.summary.kliep.md)
  :

  Print a `summary.kliep` object

- [`print(`*`<summary.kmm>`*`)`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/print.summary.kmm.md)
  :

  Print a `summary.kmm` object

- [`print(`*`<summary.lhss>`*`)`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/print.summary.lhss.md)
  :

  Print a `summary.lhss` object

- [`print(`*`<summary.naivedensityratio>`*`)`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/print.summary.naivedensityratio.md)
  :

  Print a `summary.naivedensityratio` object

- [`print(`*`<summary.spectral>`*`)`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/print.summary.spectral.md)
  :

  Print a `summary.spectral` object

- [`print(`*`<summary.ulsif>`*`)`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/print.summary.ulsif.md)
  :

  Print a `summary.ulsif` object

- [`print(`*`<ulsif>`*`)`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/print.ulsif.md)
  :

  Print a `ulsif` object

- [`spectral()`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/spectral.md)
  : Spectral series based density ratio estimation

- [`summary(`*`<kliep>`*`)`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/summary.kliep.md)
  :

  Extract summary from `kliep` object, including two-sample significance
  test for homogeneity of the numerator and denominator samples

- [`summary(`*`<kmm>`*`)`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/summary.kmm.md)
  :

  Extract summary from `kmm` object, including two-sample significance
  test for homogeneity of the numerator and denominator samples

- [`summary(`*`<lhss>`*`)`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/summary.lhss.md)
  :

  Extract summary from `lhss` object, including two-sample significance
  test for homogeneity of the numerator and denominator samples

- [`summary(`*`<naivedensityratio>`*`)`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/summary.naivedensityratio.md)
  :

  Extract summary from `naivedensityraito` object, including two-sample
  significance test for homogeneity of the numerator and denominator
  samples

- [`summary(`*`<spectral>`*`)`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/summary.spectral.md)
  :

  Extract summary from `spectral` object, including two-sample
  significance test for homogeneity of the numerator and denominator
  samples

- [`summary(`*`<ulsif>`*`)`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/summary.ulsif.md)
  :

  Extract summary from `ulsif` object, including two-sample significance
  test for homogeneity of the numerator and denominator samples

- [`ulsif()`](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/reference/ulsif.md)
  : Unconstrained least-squares importance fitting

# Articles

### All vignettes

- [Covariate shift
  adjustment](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/articles/covariate-shift.md):
- [densityratio](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/articles/densityratio.md):
- [High dimensional two-sample
  testing](https://thomvolker.github.io/densityratio/articles/high-dim-testing.md):
